model_findings.description: The `serve` subcommand accepts arbitrary `--host` values (including `0.0.0.0`) with no `--allow-remote` gate. Issue #2030 already tracks this for the bridge proxy; this new surface repeats the mistake. The proxy executes local CLI subprocesses (codex/claude/gemini/hermes) with the user's credentials and shell env, so binding to a non-loopback interface exposes RCE-equivalent capability to anyone on the network. Reject non-127.0.0.1/::1 hosts unless an explicit opt-in flag is passed.

`ChatCompletionRequest` uses `extra='allow'` and accepts `messages: list[Message]` with no max length, and `content` has no size cap. A client can POST a multi-megabyte payload that gets flattened into a single subprocess stdin string, blocking a worker for up to `_DEFAULT_BACKEND_TIMEOUT_S` (120s) per request. Combined with no concurrency limits and subprocess fan-out, a handful of large requests will pin CPU/RAM and exhaust file descriptors. Enforce max messages, max total content bytes, and a global concurrency semaphore.

`/healthz` forks four `--version` subprocesses on every request (codex/gemini/claude/hermes), each with a 1s timeout — worst case ~4s wall time and 4 processes per probe. This duplicates the exact DoS surface flagged in issue #2029 for the existing bridge proxy. Cache probe results with a short TTL (e.g. 5–10s) or expose a `?deep=1` variant and return cached state by default.

`chat_completions` is defined as a regular `def` (not `async def`) and calls `subprocess.run` with up to 120s timeout. FastAPI runs sync endpoints in a bounded threadpool (default 40 threads); a small burst of slow requests will exhaust the pool and starve `/healthz` and `/v1/models`. Either make the handler async with `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` plus a semaphore, or document and enforce a low max-concurrency.

`_gemini_backend` passes the entire flattened prompt via `--prompt=<prompt>` on argv. argv lengths are bounded by ARG_MAX (~256KB on macOS, ~2MB on Linux), so large legitimate prompts will fail with E2BIG, and the full prompt is visible in process listings (`ps`) to other local users. Codex/claude/hermes correctly use stdin; gemini should too (or `--prompt-file` with a temp file).

The companion doc states the Grok route uses `hermes -z PROMPT -m grok-4.3`, but the code invokes `hermes --oneshot=PROMPT -m model`. Either the doc or the code is wrong; given issue #2036 reports hermes auth/output problems already, this inconsistency will make debugging harder. Reconcile flags and add a test that asserts the actual argv shape (mock subprocess), not just the response.

`_codex_backend` creates a NamedTemporaryFile with `delete=False`, closes it, then passes the path to codex `-o`. If the process is killed between tempfile creation and the `finally` unlink (e.g. uvicorn worker SIGKILL), the file leaks into `/tmp`. Low impact but accumulates. Consider `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory()` context or registering an atexit cleanup.

`_backend_timeout_s` silently falls back to default on any non-int or non-positive value but logs nothing. An operator setting `BRIDGE_PROXY_BACKEND_TIMEOUT_S=600s` (with unit) will get 120s with no warning. Log a warning on parse failure.

The model is OpenAI-compatible but `extra='allow'` silently accepts `stream: true` and returns a non-streaming response, which will hang or confuse OpenAI SDK clients expecting SSE. Explicitly reject `stream=true` with a 400 in v1, matching what the doc claims ("intentionally non-streaming").

model_findings.description: The canonical 'error-correction' example printed in the failure diagnostic has identical values for `error` and `correction` ('вмиваюся' / 'вмиваюся'). A writer that copies this exemplar will produce a semantically broken item (the error and the fix are the same string). Use distinct values, e.g. `error: "вмиваюсь"` vs `correction: "вмиваюся"`, so the example actually demonstrates a corrected mistake.

When `activity.get('items')` is not a list (dict, None, scalar, or missing for a type that requires items), the gate does `continue` without recording a violation and without incrementing `checked`. A writer emitting `items: {...}` or omitting `items` entirely on an error-correction activity will pass the schema gate silently, defeating the fail-fast intent. Either require `items` to be a non-empty list for typed activities or emit an explicit violation for malformed `items` containers.

The alias map enumerates a finite list of plausible-looking field names (wrong/incorrect/mistake/correctAnswer/right/fix/...). Real writer regressions also produce variants like `errorForm`, `wrongAnswer`, `corrected`, `solution`, `target`, `expected`, `fixed_form`. These are still rejected as forbidden (good — they're not in the whitelist), but without the helpful 'use X: instead' remediation hint, so the writer correction loop loses the most useful signal. Consider expanding the alias map, or adding a fuzzy/regex fallback that maps any field containing 'wrong|incorrect|mistake|error' → 'error' and 'correct|fix|right|answer|solution' → 'correction' for diagnostics.

`activity_schema` is added to WRITER_CORRECTION_GATES, but there is no visible update to the writer-correction prompt template or context builder in this diff that explains the new gate's canonical schema to the model. Verify that the correction-round prompt actually surfaces `_format_activity_schema_diagnostic`'s message (it should, since it's stored in `report['message']`), and that the prompt template doesn't filter it out. Otherwise the writer will see 'activity_schema failed' with no remediation guidance.
